meta.description: Reference implementation of programmatic build-journey tracking for the QWF Advisor Brain under the Quietly Working Foundation's Quietly Operating Standard (QOS). Companion to QOS.development-map.json: where QOS clarifies WHO an organization is, the Brain map tracks the build of HOW it decides. Public at brain.quietlyos.org. Maintained by Quietly Working Foundation (501(c)(3)).

changelog.change: Initial BRAIN.development-map.json with 27 nodes across 6 phases. Schema reuse: this instance validates against qos_development_map.v1.schema.json (no Brain-specific fork in Phase 0). Phase 0 (Origin & Foundations) seeded with 6 shipped nodes pointing at real vault artifacts (master capture, OS+Brain companion framing, three-verdict reinforcement loop, role-based reviewer design call, share-not-sell voice discipline, site launch plan) plus 1 planned (the public-site Phase 0 launch). Phases 1-5 seeded with 21 planned nodes covering the schema layer (decisions.v1 + report format + append helper), runtime (5 nodes), reinforcement UI (4 nodes), calibration (4 nodes), and open standard (4 nodes).
changelog.reason: Initial author. Brain dev map shipped same day as the OS+Brain split was locked in memory and the site launch plan was captured; the map is the third leg making the architecture machine-readable.
changelog.author: TIG + Claude (Opus 4.7)

changelog.change: p0.brain.site-phase-0 toggled planned -> shipped. brain.quietlyos.org live with valid SSL (Google Trust Services, valid through 2026-07-26); www and .com variants 301 to canonical; build map renders 6 of 27 nodes shipped (22%); em-dash scrub plus supporter-anonymity sweep plus youth-protection sweep framework plus TIG-personal toggle all clean on live HTML. CF Pages deploy via GitHub Actions wrangler-action (45s build). Hand-edit toggle (Python toggle script not yet generalized for Brain JSON; commit history preserves the transition).
changelog.reason: E2E verification complete; the Brain has its public surface. Phase 0 closes; Phase 1 (decisions.v1 schema + report-format + append-script) is the natural next sequence.
changelog.author: Brain Phase 0 Build Session (Claude Opus 4.7) plus TIG verdicts

changelog.change: Phase 1 schema layer shipped: p1.brain.decisions-v1, p1.brain.report-format, p1.brain.append-script all toggled planned -> shipped. decisions.v1 schema (22 $defs, QOS meta, em-dash-free) plus directive plus QWF root ledger plus examples file plus validator plus resolver plus append_decision.py canonical writer plus C4 E2E smoke test verified end-to-end against live hq_brain_verdicts table and #advisor-brain Discord channel. Build map count: 7 of 27 -> 10 of 27 (37%).
changelog.reason: Phase 1 of the QWF Advisor Brain build plan complete. The schema layer unblocks Phase 2 (the headless runtime that consumes the schema).
changelog.author: Phase 1 decisions.v1 Build Session (Claude Opus 4.7) plus TIG verdicts

changelog.change: Phase 3 Brain MVP runtime shipped: p2.brain.headless-runner, p2.brain.corpus-query, p2.brain.surprise-pause, p2.brain.commit-staging all toggled planned -> shipped. Runner.py + corpus_query.py + brain_runtime_config.json + run_brain_overnight.sh built and verified via C4 rehearsal: 27-turn autonomous build executed against synthetic plan, $2.61 inside $8 cap, all wired tool paths fired, trust-weight dual-write split honored (NOVEL via append_decision, SOFT-PREFERENCE via write_brain_verdict), em-dash discipline held. Four self-catches surfaced honestly: query() vs ClaudeSDKClient for SDK MCP servers, max_thinking_tokens incompatible with Opus 4.7, agent build-report false-positive on self-catch (system prompt fix), top_calls schema mismatch in checkpoint formatter (formatter fix). Build map count: 10 of 27 -> 14 of 27 (52%).
changelog.reason: Phase 3 of the QWF Advisor Brain build plan complete. The runtime exists, exercises every wired tool, respects all C1 verdicts, and passes its own self-catch discipline. Phase 4 (mobile UX polish, trust meter sub-views) and Phase 5 (open standard publication) are the natural next sequences.
changelog.author: Phase 3 Brain MVP Runtime Build Session (Claude Opus 4.7) plus TIG verdicts

changelog.change: p2.brain.first-overnight-run toggled planned -> shipped. First real overnight run executed against the Phase 4 ask-question-gesture design build plan. Runtime completed 5m 49s, 39 turns, $5.78 of $8 cap, four cards landed clean (2 NOVEL via append_decision dual-write + 2 SOFT-PREFERENCE via write_brain_verdict direct), zero self-catches, zero crash-loud events. All four cards approved-silent by TIG at 08:03-08:05 Pacific 2026-04-29 after the 8am morning digest surfaced them. Operational replacement loop ran end-to-end for the first time: Brain designed, TIG slept, morning digest surfaced, TIG verdicted, advisor session synced. Build map count: 14 of 27 -> 15 of 27 (56%).
changelog.reason: First-light is the proof of MVP per the dev map's own description. The Brain ran autonomously, produced real architectural output (a 274-line Phase 4 design spec implementation-ready for D4.1-D4.5 sequencing), respected every C1 lock (TC1-TC4), and earned a clean approved-silent verdict on every card. Operational replacement of routine architectural calls is real, demonstrated, and durable across a sleep cycle.
changelog.author: Phase 4 First-Light Run + TIG Morning Verdicts

phases.label: Origin & Foundations
phases.description: What the Brain stands on before code. Master capture, OS+Brain companion framing locked in memory, three-verdict reinforcement loop, role-based reviewer design call, share-not-sell voice discipline, public-site launch plan. Phase 0 is the architectural ground truth.
phases.goal: Every load-bearing concept later phases consume has a vault artifact grounding it; each shipped node passes the drift validator against a real file.

phases.label: Schema Layer
phases.description: decisions.v1 schema, the report-format template, the append-decision helper script. The JSON contract for every Brain entry, plus the writer.
phases.goal: decisions.v1 schema shipped + validator + helper + report-format template; ready for the runtime to call.

phases.label: Runtime
phases.description: The headless Claude Agent SDK runner that reads a build plan, queries the corpus at decision time, logs WHY, pauses on novel decisions and escalates to HQ, stages commits without pushing. The Brain becomes load-bearing here.
phases.goal: First overnight run completes successfully, ledger entries persist, surprises queue cleanly, no autonomous commits land in main.

phases.label: Reinforcement UI
phases.description: The interface that closes the loop. Batch review surface, three-verdict mechanism (Approved / Approved+Reinforcement / Redirected+Reinforcement), trust-meter calculation, corpus update on redirect.
phases.goal: Every overnight run produces a report TIG (or designated reviewer) can verdict in 5 to 10 minutes; verdicts update the corpus deterministically.

phases.label: Calibration
phases.description: Per-category trust meters, stale-corpus detection, random-sample spot-check on high-trust decisions, prompt-injection integrity protections.
phases.goal: Trust behavior matches reality; no silent drift; no poisoned corpus; integrity checks routine.

phases.label: Open Standard
phases.description: The Brain pattern published as a public QOS reference implementation. Documentation, governance license, adopt-page with fork instructions, first supporter adopter.
phases.goal: Another organization can fork the Brain pattern using only what is published at brain.quietlyos.org and the QOS schema gallery.

nodes.label: Master Architectural Capture
nodes.description: Captures the full Brain architecture: Strategy C autonomous-overnight orchestrator, three-verdict reinforcement loop, decision-ledger schema sketch, anti-patterns, success metrics, integration points with existing systems, phased build plan.
nodes.why: Architecture must survive context compaction. The capture is the canonical source of truth that future agents (and supporters) read first.

files.description: Full architecture document including OS+Brain companion framing, three-verdict reinforcement loop, anti-patterns, success metrics.

nodes.label: OS + Brain Companion Framing
nodes.description: Locks the Brain as the operational HOW-layer that complements QOS. QOS clarifies WHO an organization is (schemas); Brain clarifies HOW it decides (choices + WHY). Wisdom Library is a separate artifact handling what the org KNOWS, not part of the Brain's territory.
nodes.why: Without an explicit companion framing, the Brain risked being mis-shelved as the QOS Wisdom Layer. That conflation flattened two distinct artifacts and weakened both. Locking the framing in memory protects against future drift.

files.description: Master memory file: description, the three-things-at-once frame, OS+Brain split, public home brain.quietlyos.org.

nodes.label: Three-Verdict Reinforcement Loop
nodes.description: Locks the three verdict states (Approved / Approved+Reinforcement / Redirected+Reinforcement) and their effects on corpus and trust meter. Names the redirect itself as a reinforcement event when paired with WHY.
nodes.why: Without locked verdict semantics, the corpus would drift on what counts as 'approval' and what counts as 'teaching.' Naming the three states explicitly makes the loop's compounding mechanic legible to any future reviewer or adopter.

files.description: Section 'Three-Verdict Reinforcement Loop' with the full table of corpus + trust meter effects.

nodes.label: Role-Based Reviewer Design
nodes.description: Forward-looking design call: decisions.v1 schema must support multiple reviewers and role-based routing from day one. A future supporter adopting the Brain might have a small board, not a single founder. Schema needs reviewer (role + identity) and verdict.signed_by from the start.
nodes.why: Cheap to design in now, painful to retrofit. TIG named 'decision making member or team' when describing who reviews reports... that phrase is load-bearing for adopter supporters with multi-person decision rights.

files.description: Section 'Role-Based Reviewer Support' captures the forward-looking design constraint.

nodes.label: Share-Not-Sell Voice Discipline
nodes.description: Named corpus preference enforcing no-competitive-framing on all public-facing QWF content. Frame work as 'what fits our weaknesses and strengths,' never 'better than what others do.' Underneath: invitation, not argument. Reader's first job is Know Thyself.
nodes.why: Voice discipline gets harder to retrofit once content ships at scale. Locking the rule before brain.quietlyos.org publishes prevents the agentic team from drifting into adversarial framing that violates the Quietly Working brand anchor.

files.description: Named corpus preference with five-point WHY, application notes, severity, scope, and the live-redirect demonstration that reinforced it.

nodes.label: brain.quietlyos.org Launch Plan
nodes.description: Phased launch plan for the public Brain home. Separate-repo decision (QuietlyWorking/quietly-brain), CF Pages + SvelteKit, subdomain CNAME, six Phase 0 sections, seven new components, day-one node tree, three-sweep anonymity discipline, mission-thesis hero copy locked.
nodes.why: Without a documented launch plan, the fresh-session agent that builds Phase 0 would have to extrapolate scope, voice, and architecture from scratch. The plan turns build-in-public from intent into executable sequence.

files.description: Launch plan for brain.quietlyos.org including phase rollout, infrastructure spec, components, anonymity, day-one node tree, hero copy, next-actions.

nodes.label: brain.quietlyos.org Phase 0 Public Launch
nodes.description: Build and ship the public Phase 0 surface of brain.quietlyos.org per the launch plan. Six sections (hero, how-it-works, architecture, build map, what-goes-here-next, open standard), seven components, supporter sweep + em-dash scrub at build, CF Pages deploy, DNS CNAME, SSL, cross-link with quietlyos.org footer.
nodes.why: Build-in-public requires a public surface. The launch plan describes the destination; this node ships the actual site. Without this node, the Brain stays internal and the transparency story misses its central artifact.

files.description: SvelteKit + adapter-cloudflare site shipped to brain.quietlyos.org via Cloudflare Pages project quietly-brain. Repo: https://github.com/QuietlyWorking/quietly-brain. Six Phase 0 sections plus footer; em-dash scrub plus supporter-anonymity sweep plus youth-protection sweep framework plus TIG-personal toggle wired at build.
Anti-compaction memory documenting deployed infrastructure (CF Pages project, 4 DNS CNAMEs, Google Trust Services SSL, build-time sweeps, sync rule, known issues).
End-of-session build report. 6 TOP CALLS pre-reviewed; first fresh-session execution against the Brain Phase 0 kickoff handoff. Self-catches surfaced.

nodes.label: decisions.v1 Schema
nodes.description: JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) defining the decision-ledger entry shape: id, context, call, WHY-at-time-of-decision (required), expected outcome, trust weight (HARD-RULE / SOFT-PREFERENCE / NOVEL), verdict (pending / approved / approved-with-reinforcement / redirected), feedback (TIG's WHY on redirect), trace (commits, files, HQ items, dev-map nodes), reviewer (role + identity), verdict.signed_by.
nodes.why: Without a schema, ledger entries drift in shape; future analysis and reinforcement-loop tooling break. Schema also enables open-standard publication: a supporter can adopt the Brain pattern using the same JSON contract.
nodes.notes: Cross-instance dependency on QOS p1.content.v1 (already shipped 2026-04-26). Brain decisions reason in the language content.v1 defines.

files.description: JSON Schema Draft 2020-12; 22 $defs; QOS reference-implementation meta block; conditional root validation; per-entry conditional rules for kind / verdict-state combinations; structured Reviewer with role + identity; CrossSchemaRef pattern reused from content.v1; em-dash-free schema prose.

SOP for the schema. Goal / Trigger / Inputs / Tools / Steps / Outputs / Edge Cases / Implementation Status / Changelog. Documents schema-to-table mapping, WHY-at-time-of-decision discipline, append-only invariant, multi-org cascade for supporter forks.
QWF foundation root ledger. entries[] empty (Phase 2 runtime populates); 8 trust-meter categories seeded; reviewerRoles[] founder-only; meta.purpose=ledger.

Doc-only sample entries (meta.purpose=examples). Three entries demonstrating: (1) NOVEL plus approved-with-reinforcement plus full trace; (2) SOFT-PREFERENCE plus silent approval; (3) hypothetical kind=promotion plus HARD-RULE.

nodes.label: Report Format Template
nodes.description: Locked template for end-of-session Brain reports. Required: Non-technical description first per call, top-N call detail, action table at end, open-questions section, cross-build observations. Format requirement no-silent-approval-during-formation enforced until trust meters stabilize.
nodes.why: The report is the human interface for the reinforcement loop. Format quality determines whether feedback compounds the corpus or fatigues the reviewer. Locking the template before Brain MVP runs prevents drift during the corpus-formation period.

files.description: Master capture §The Report Format documents the locked template: Non-technical description first per call, Items Awaiting Explicit Verdict section, top-N call detail with WHY-at-decision, alternatives, suggested verdict.
Phase 0 fresh-session execution build report. First demonstration of the locked template; carried 6 TOP CALLS, 3 self-catches, action table, open questions.

Phase 1 build report shipped this session. Second demonstration of the locked template; carries TOP CALLS per checkpoint, self-catches surfaced during the run, action table, open questions awaiting verdict.

nodes.label: append_decision.py Helper
nodes.description: Python helper script that appends a decisions.v1 entry to the ledger with WHY, trust weight, and trace. Validates against decisions.v1 schema before write. Returns structured result.
nodes.why: Deterministic ledger writes prevent corpus corruption. Helper script is the canonical interface; agents call it instead of hand-editing JSON files.

files.description: Canonical writer. Validates proposed entry against decisions.v1 schema; writes HQ first via existing write_brain_verdict.py; links HQ row id back into entry via trace.hqItems cross-reference; writes canonical JSON; rolls back HQ row if JSON write fails. --dry-run + --skip-hq flags for diagnostics.

Validator. Structural (jsonschema) + cross-reference integrity + em-dash leak detector + required-WHY check + filename-vs-purpose consistency. Examples files surface cross-ref misses as warnings; ledger files block.

Resolver. Cascade child over parent per the locked policy (entries locked, categories/reviewerRoles/agentContext additive-or-override). Cross-schema reference annotation in _resolution.lineage. --check-cross-refs standalone mode.

C4 E2E smoke test. Exercises full pipeline against live infrastructure: append plus PostgREST read-back plus Discord webhook (?wait=true for message-id verification) plus canonical round-trip plus cleanup. All 5 steps verified far-end delivery per feedback_e2e_means_inbox_not_db.md.

nodes.label: Headless Claude Agent SDK Runner
nodes.description: Long-running Claude Agent SDK process that reads a build plan, executes checkpoints, calls append_decision.py at every decision point, surfaces escalations to HQ, stages commits without pushing.
nodes.why: Overnight runs require a runtime that survives without a chat session attached. The SDK is the substrate; this node makes it Brain-aware.

files.description: Headless Claude Agent SDK runner. ClaudeSDKClient context-manager pattern (query() does not initialize SDK MCP servers correctly). One session per overnight build (TC1 lock). Six custom tools registered via in-process MCP server. AgentDefinition for parallel sub-agent spawn (TC2 lock). PreToolUse self-catch hook on Write/Edit. Build report writer with self-catch refusal.

Runtime config encoding all C1 verdicts. TC1 session lifecycle, TC2 spawn policy, TC3 trust-weight write split, TC4 cost cap with telemetry. max_thinking_tokens dropped per Opus 4.7 incompatibility... Opus 4.7 native adaptive thinking carries TC4 quality intent.
Cron-friendly invocation wrapper. Pre-flight env checks, .env sourcing, exit codes for build complete vs guardrail vs pre-flight failure.

nodes.label: Corpus Query at Decision Time
nodes.description: Decision-time lookup against memory/feedback_*.md files plus prior decision-ledger entries. Returns relevant SOFT PREFERENCES and precedent decisions for the Brain to reason against.
nodes.why: Without precedent lookup, the Brain re-decides every call from scratch. With it, the Brain operates on accumulated judgment, which is the entire point of the corpus.

files.description: Decision-time corpus lookup. Eight actions: list / find / read / redirects / precedent / pending / trust / hq-pending. Reads memory/feedback_*.md, QWF.decisions.json, hq_brain_verdicts. Wrapped as @tool for SDK consumption. CLI dispatcher for diagnostics.

nodes.label: Surprise Pause + HQ Escalation
nodes.description: When the Brain hits a NOVEL decision (not in corpus, not a HARD RULE), it pauses the run, files an HQ item with full context, and waits for TIG's verdict. No autonomous resolution of novel calls. Refined during Phase 3 build: rather than literally pausing the run on NOVEL decisions, the runner logs them as pending verdicts (via append_decision dual-write) and continues. The reviewer verdicts asynchronously on the Brain page. Functionally equivalent to the original spec... no autonomous resolution of novel calls, every NOVEL gets a card, the corpus only grows via reviewer-blessed verdicts. Aligns with the Phase 2A escalation-contract refinement (chat-report+card pattern replaces blocking pause).
nodes.why: Cold-start treatment for novel decisions is the safety net. It prevents the Brain from inventing precedent and lets the corpus grow only via TIG-blessed entries.

nodes.label: Commit Staging (No Push)
nodes.description: Brain stages commits but never pushes to main. TIG green-lights pushes during morning review. The zero-autonomous-commits gate from CLAUDE.md applies absolutely.
nodes.why: Reversibility is the safety property. A staged commit can be amended, squashed, or discarded; a pushed commit is public history. Gate stays even when trust meter is high.

files.description: System prompt forbids git commit / push. Sub-agents inherit the same gate. C4 rehearsal verified: 27 turns of agent execution staged file changes via Edit/Write but never invoked git commit or git push.

nodes.label: First Successful Overnight Run
nodes.description: Inaugural autonomous overnight run executes a real build plan (likely a future schema checkpoint), produces ledger entries, queues HQ escalations as needed, stages commits. Morning review verifies the run via the report format.
nodes.why: First-light is the proof of MVP. Without a successful overnight, every later phase is theoretical. With it, the Brain becomes load-bearing.

files.description: First-light overnight design output: 274-line Phase 4 polish spec for the ask-question gesture on HQ Brain page cards. Covers data model (4 nullable cols on hq_brain_verdicts), gesture (swipe-up), routing (existing realtime UPDATE channel), Lovable prompt 145 skeleton, advisor session integration, 9 edge cases, build sequence D4.1-D4.5, self-consistency check.

First-light build report. 5m 49s, 39 turns, $5.78 (72% of $8 cap), 4 cards landed (2 NOVEL via append_decision dual-write + 2 SOFT-PREFERENCE via write_brain_verdict direct), zero self-catches, zero crashes. All 4 cards approved-silent by TIG at 08:03-08:05 Pacific 2026-04-29 after the 8am morning digest fired.
Phase 4 first-light build plan. Specifies the 4-checkpoint design exercise; gitignored as part of .tmp/.

nodes.label: Batch Review Surface (HQ Filter View)
nodes.description: HQ Command Center filter view that surfaces every Brain ledger entry awaiting verdict, grouped by session, with the report format pre-rendered. TIG (or designated reviewer) verdicts in 5 to 10 minutes.
nodes.why: Reviewer fatigue kills the reinforcement loop. A purpose-built batch review surface keeps verdict cost low and keeps the corpus growing.

nodes.label: Three-Verdict Mechanism
nodes.description: Reviewer interface that captures Approved / Approved+Reinforcement (with WHY) / Redirected+Reinforcement (with WHY). TIG-only signature. Append-only ledger; no edits to past verdicts.
nodes.why: The mechanism enforces the locked three-verdict semantics from p0.brain.three-verdict-loop. Without it, reinforcement happens informally and inconsistently.

nodes.label: Trust Meter Calculation
nodes.description: Per-decision-category agreement-rate over the last 20 decisions. High (90%+) auto-handles, medium (70-89%) flags for spot-check, low (<70%) re-escalates. Subcategorization prevents over-generalization.
nodes.why: The trust meter is the calibration signal. Without it, the Brain cannot distinguish stable categories from drift-prone ones, and TIG cannot see drift before it becomes habit.

nodes.label: Corpus Update on Redirect
nodes.description: When a verdict is Redirected+Reinforcement, the writer updates the relevant memory file with TIG's WHY. Append for new categories, rewrite for existing rules. Decrement trust meter.
nodes.why: The redirect-with-WHY is where the corpus grows. Without an automated writer, redirects stay in chat and never compound. With it, every redirect makes the next session smarter.

nodes.label: Per-Category Trust Subdivision
nodes.description: Subdivide trust meter into specific decision shapes (e.g., 'cascade naming for forbidden patterns' vs 'cascade naming for vocabulary alternatives') so high agreement in one shape does not inflate trust in adjacent shapes.
nodes.why: Trust inflation is a documented anti-pattern. Subcategorization is the structural prevention.

nodes.label: Stale Corpus Detection (90-Day Rule)
nodes.description: Every memory file gets a last-reinforced timestamp. Decisions in categories not reinforced for 90 days re-escalate to TIG. Stale rules expire safely instead of compounding silently.
nodes.why: Corpus drift (TIG's preferences evolve away from a stored rule) is invisible without a re-escalation trigger. The 90-day rule forces refresh.

nodes.label: Random-Sample Spot-Check on High-Trust Decisions
nodes.description: Every batch report random-samples a few HIGH-TRUST decisions for explicit verdict ('the Brain says this was 95% confidence... do you actually agree?'). Track over-approval as its own metric.
nodes.why: Silent disagreement (TIG approves something he secretly disagreed with) is the hardest anti-pattern to detect. Random spot-checks surface it.

nodes.label: Prompt Injection Integrity Checks
nodes.description: Signed ledger entries (agent-id + commit hash). Append-only ledger. TIG-only verdict signatures. Random-sample integrity checks against known-clean baseline.
nodes.why: A poisoned ledger entry could fake precedent and shape future decisions. Integrity checks make tampering detectable.

nodes.label: Brain Pattern Documentation
nodes.description: Public documentation of the Brain pattern as a QOS reference implementation. Lives at brain.quietlyos.org; covers schema, runtime, reinforcement loop, anti-patterns, success metrics, fork-it instructions.
nodes.why: Open-standard publication is the multiplier. Documenting the pattern lets supporter organizations adopt it without QWF re-explaining for every adopter.

nodes.label: Governance License
nodes.description: Open-source license under which the Brain pattern (schema + reference implementation) is published. Aligns with the broader QOS license decision (currently '[pending governance]').
nodes.why: Without a license, supporter forks live in legal ambiguity. The license sets the terms under which adoption is welcomed.

nodes.label: Adopt-The-Brain Page
nodes.description: Public page on brain.quietlyos.org with concrete fork-it instructions: clone the schema, scaffold a corpus, plug into your decision rights structure, run your first batch review. Includes worked examples from QWF (anonymized) and any consenting supporter adopters.
nodes.why: Documentation alone does not lower adoption cost; a fork-it walkthrough does. The adopt page is the difference between 'read about this' and 'try this tonight.'

nodes.label: First Supporter Fork
nodes.description: First non-QWF organization adopts the Brain pattern using the published schema + adopt page. Their corpus stays private; HARD RULES inherited from QOS public standard; SOFT PREFERENCES are theirs.
nodes.why: Adoption is the ultimate proof of standard. A supporter running their own Brain validates that the pattern transfers, not just the implementation.

milestones.label: Schema Layer Complete
milestones.description: decisions.v1 schema, report-format template, and append helper all shipped. The Brain has a JSON contract and a writer.

milestones.label: Runtime + Reinforcement Operational
milestones.description: First overnight run completed and verdicted via the three-verdict mechanism. The Brain is load-bearing; the corpus compounds across sessions.

milestones.label: Brain v1 Complete
milestones.description: Every p1 through p5 node shipped. Calibration is routine. Open standard published. First supporter has forked. The Brain pattern is a transferable QOS reference implementation.
